34: eloi eloi lema sabachthani — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — Mark uses a slightly different Aramaic form from Matthew; both transliterate Ps 22:1. The double divine address (my God, my God) is the Psalm's opening cry — urgent, trusting, and anguished simultaneously. Mark's stark account of the crucifixion (no earthquake, no opened tombs as in Matthew, no thief-repentance as in Luke) makes the cry of dereliction the climactic statement: the Son of God dies abandoned by God, bearing what sinners deserve, before the vindication that Ps 22's own ending provides (vv.24-31: YHWH has not despised the affliction of the afflicted one).
39: alethos houtos ho anthropos huios en theou — truly this man was Son of God — the centurion's confession is the human climax of Mark's Christology: the one who was declared Son of God by the Father at the baptism (1:11) is recognized as Son of God by a Roman soldier at the death. The inclusio is complete. Mark has no birth narrative because he begins with the Son's public identification (baptism-declaration) and ends with the soldier's confession — the entire Gospel is bounded by the Son of God identity, revealed through the Servant's death rather than despite it.
1: prōi / symboylion hetoimasantes / dēsantes / Pilatō paredōkan — very early / having made a plan / having bound / handed over to Pilate — prōi (very early morning — the fourth watch, before dawn). Symboylion hetoimasantes (having made/prepared a plan) — the formal council decision for delivery to Roman authority. Dēsantes (having bound Jesus) — the binding that continues from the arrest (14:46). Paredōkan (handed over — aorist of paradidōmi) — the chain of betrayal reaches Pilate: from Judas (14:10) to the priests (14:53) to Pilate. Hēgemōni (governor — the Roman title for the prefect of Judea).
2: sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn / sy legeis — are you the king of the Jews? / you say it — basileus tōn Ioudaiōn (King of the Jews) — the Roman political charge, appearing five times in Mark 15 (vv.2, 9, 12, 18, 26). Sy legeis (you say so / that is your word) — an affirmation but not a straightforward yes. The construction is the same as in 14:62 where Jesus replied to a different question with egō eimi. Here the response is more distancing: the title is Pilate's framing, not Jesus's preferred category. Some translators render it as acknowledgment; others as subtle qualification.
3: katēgoroun / polla — were accusing / many things — the imperfect katēgoroun (were accusing, kept accusing — continuous action) pictures sustained pressure from the chief priests. Polla (many things) without specification — the plural accusations are left vague; what matters narratively is that Jesus does not respond to any of them (v.5). The charges were presumably expanded from the Sanhedrin proceedings (14:55–64) into Roman political language.
4: oudeno apokriē / posa sou katamarturousin — you answer nothing / how many things they testify against you — Pilate's astonishment at Jesus's silence is introduced with his second direct question. The cumulative weight of multiple accusers pressing charges while the accused stands mute is extraordinary. Isaiah 53:7 (the silent servant) provides the scriptural frame, though Mark does not cite it explicitly here.
5: ouketi ouden apekrinatō / thaumazein — he answered nothing more / to be amazed — ouketi ouden (no longer anything — emphatic double negation). Thaumazein ton Pilaton (Pilate to be amazed) — thaumazō is Mark's word for Jesus's own astonishment at unbelief (6:6) and for the crowds' amazement at his teaching. Here Pilate the pagan administrator is amazed at Jesus's self-mastery. The reversal is pointed: the one with Roman authority is confounded by the prisoner.
6: apolyen auton hena desmon / paretounto — he would release one prisoner / they would request — apolyen (imperfect — he used to release, it was his custom); the construction describes the Passover amnesty as a repeating practice. Desmon (prisoner, bound one). The historicity of this custom has been debated — it is not attested in Roman or Jewish sources outside the Gospels — but may represent a local accommodation to Jewish festival expectations.
7: Barabbas / stasiastais / phonon pepoiēkei en tē stasei — Barabbas / insurrectionists / had committed murder in the insurrection — Barabbas is Aramaic bar Abbas (son of Abbas or son of the father — abba). The naming choice creates a profound irony: the crowd releases a murderous bar Abbas (son of the father) and demands the death of the one who truly is the Son of the Father. Stasiastai (rebels, insurrectionists) and phonos (murder) — Barabbas is the genuine political criminal that Rome accused Jesus of being.
8: anaboēsas / parekalei — crying out / began to ask — anaboēsas (crying out, going up crying — the ana- prefix may indicate the crowd surging forward). The imperfect parekalei (were asking) describes the crowd petitioning Pilate to honor the release custom. The crowd's demand is distinct from the chief priests' manipulation (v.11) — they initially only want the customary release, not specifically the death of Jesus.
9: thelete apolyso hymin ton basilea tōn Ioudaiōn — do you want me to release to you the King of the Jews? — Pilate offers Jesus using the title the priests used in the charge. The question is politically loaded: accepting the release implicitly endorses the Messianic claim the priests are trying to suppress. The crowd's eventual rejection of this offer is engineered by the priests (v.11), not spontaneous.
10: eginōsken hoti dia phthonon — he knew that out of envy — phthonos (envy, jealous resentment — one of the vices listed in Paul's catalogs, e.g. Rom 1:29; Gal 5:21; the word for the malicious begrudging of another's status or success). Pilate reads the priests' motive correctly: Jesus's popularity threatens their institutional power. This is the narrator's direct attribution of motive, a rare moment of interiority given to the Roman governor.
11: aneseisan ton ochlon — stirred up the crowd — anaseiō (to shake up, agitate, stir — a vivid verb for rabble-rousing; ana- = upward motion + seiō = to shake). The chief priests actively work the crowd to redirect the Passover amnesty demand toward Barabbas. The manipulation is openly narrated: the crowd's eventual cry is orchestrated, not organic.
12: ti oun poiēsō... ton basilea tōn Ioudaiōn — what then shall I do with the King of the Jews? — Pilate's question uses the royal title again, perhaps deliberately provoking the crowd. The three-way political trap: Pilate cannot release a claimed king without appearing to endorse sedition; he cannot condemn an innocent man without injustice; he cannot satisfy the priests without becoming their instrument. His solution — letting the crowd decide — abdicates judicial responsibility.
13: staurosōn / stauron — crucify! / cross — stauroō (to crucify) from stauros (stake, post, cross). Crucifixion was designed for maximum suffering and public deterrence — reserved for slaves, non-Roman criminals, and political rebels. The naked victim was fixed (usually nailed) to the cross and died from a combination of exposure, dehydration, and asphyxiation. The crowd's demand requires no legal justification in their answer — just the demand.
14: ti gar epoiēsen kakon / perissōs ekraxan — what evil has he done? / all the more loudly they shouted — Pilate's question (ti kakon epoiēsen, what evil/harm did he do?) is the closest thing to an acquittal in the Markan trial narrative. He finds no basis for the charge but lacks the moral authority to act on this finding. Perissōs (exceedingly, all the more — an intensifier from perisseuo, to exceed, to abound).
15: to hikanon poiēsai / phragellōsas / paredōken — to satisfy / having flogged / handed over — to hikanon poiēsai (to do enough, to satisfy — a Latinism: satisfacere rendered in Greek) — legal terminology for satisfying a legal demand. Phragellōsas (having flogged — from Latin flagellum, the Roman military scourging whip; leather thongs embedded with bone and metal fragments that could lacerate flesh to the bone). The flogging preceded the crucifixion and often killed the condemned before they reached the cross. Paredōken (handed over) — the final link in the paradidōmi chain.
16: Praitōrion / holēn tēn speiran — the Praetorium / the whole cohort — Praitōrion (Latin praetorium) — the governor's official residence and headquarters in Jerusalem, either the Antonia Fortress adjacent to the temple or Herod's palace. Speira (cohort — a Roman military unit of approximately 480-600 soldiers; one tenth of a legion). The phrase holēn tēn speiran (the whole cohort) may be hyperbolic, suggesting a large mocking assembly.
17: porphyran / stephanos ex akanthōn — purple (robe) / crown of thorns — porphyra (purple cloth — the color of imperial and royal robes in Rome; Tyrian purple dye was extraordinarily expensive). The soldiers mock the Messianic claim with the visible symbols of kingship: robe and crown. Akanthōn (thorns) — the crown may have used Ziziphus spina-christi (Christ-thorn) or Poterium spinosum, common thorny plants of Palestine. The mock coronation inverts every element of royal investiture.
18: chaire, basileu tōn Ioudaiōn — Hail, King of the Jews! — chaire (Hail! / Greetings! — the standard Greek salutation) parodies the Roman imperial acclamation Ave Caesar (Hail Caesar). The soldiers perform a satirical coronation ceremony, complete with the royal greeting, the purple robe, and the crown. In performing the mockery, they unknowingly enact the truth of the title they ridicule.
19: ekalamō etypton autou tēn kephalēn / eneptyon autō / gonypetountes prosekunoun autō — they beat his head with a reed / spat on him / falling on knees worshiped him — The triad of humiliation: striking with the mock scepter (the reed), spitting (the gesture of ultimate contempt), and parodic worship (proskuneō — the genuine word for kneeling adoration). The soldiers complete the scene of Isaiah 53:3 (despised and rejected) while performing the gestures that are theologically due to the one they are mocking.
20: enepaixan / exagousin auton hina staurōsōsin auton — they mocked / they lead him out to crucify him — empazō (to mock, play with, treat as a toy) — the mockery complete; now the execution. The shift from imperfect to present tense (exagousin, they lead out — vivid historical present) accelerates the narrative pace. The victim was typically made to carry the horizontal beam (patibulum) of his cross to the execution site.
21: Kyrēnaion / Alexandrou kai Rhouphoy patera / agorogeuousin — a Cyrenian / father of Alexander and Rufus / they press into service — Kyrēnaios (from Cyrene — a North African city with a large Jewish diaspora community). Mark identifies Simon by his sons: Alexandrou kai Rhouphoy patera (father of Alexander and Rufus) — this parenthetical identifies Simon to a community that knew his sons. Romans 16:13 mentions a Rufus in Rome, possibly the same person. Agoregeuousin (they press into compulsory service — a military requisition term, from Latin angaria).
22: Golgothon / Kranion Topos — Golgotha / Place of the Skull — Golgothon is Greek transliteration of Aramaic Gulgolta (skull). Mark translates for his audience: Kranion Topos (Place of Skull — kranion is the Greek anatomical term for skull, root of the English cranium). The name likely described the rocky outcrop's shape. The site was apparently a public execution location outside the city walls (Heb 13:12) on a main road, maximizing visibility as deterrence.
23: esmyrnismenon oinon / ouk elaben — wine mixed with myrrh / he did not take it — smyrna (myrrh — a bitter resinous gum from the Commiphora tree, used medicinally and as an analgesic). Wine with myrrh was offered as a mild painkiller or narcotic before crucifixion. Jesus's refusal (ouk elaben, he did not take) is deliberate — he chooses to face the cross fully conscious. The refusal is consistent with the Gethsemane prayer's eventual submission: he will drink the cup his Father gives him (14:36).
24: staurousin auton / diamerizontai ta himatia / ballon klēron — they crucified him / they divide his garments / casting lots — Mark records the crucifixion itself in three words: kai staurousin auton (and they crucified him) — no elaboration of the physical details. The narrative moves immediately to the division of garments, a Psalm 22:18 echo: diemerisanto ta himatia mou heautois kai epi ton himatismon mou ebalon klēron (they divided my garments among themselves and cast lots for my clothing). The Scripture is enacted without being cited.
25: hōra tritē / estaurōsan auton — the third hour / they crucified him — the third hour in Jewish/Roman daytime reckoning is approximately 9 AM (counting from sunrise). This differs from John 19:14, which places the trial still underway at the sixth hour (noon). The discrepancy has generated extensive discussion; most scholars treat it as a difference in timekeeping conventions or in the evangelist's rhetorical purposes. Mark's third-hour crucifixion allows for three hours of darkness (noon to 3 PM, vv.33–37).
26: epigraphē tēs aitias / HO BASILEUS TŌN IOUDAIŌN — the written notice of the charge / THE KING OF THE JEWS — epigraphē (inscription, written notice) — the titulus (Latin): a placard specifying the condemned's crime, typically carried before him in procession and then fixed above the cross. The charge is not blasphemy (the Sanhedrin's verdict) but sedition — claiming royal authority. In Roman law, this was the capital crime. The irony: the charge is the truth written above his head.
27: lēstas / ek dexiōn / ex euōnymōn — bandits/rebels / on his right / on his left — lēstai (bandits, rebels — the same word Jesus used at his arrest: as against a lēstēn, 14:48). The placement between two rebels echoes Isaiah 53:12 (meta anomōn elogisthē, he was numbered with the transgressors) — a connection made explicit in v.28 (the textual note below). The positioning ek dexiōn / ex euōnymōn (right / left) ironically fulfills the disciples' request for the seats of honor at his right and left (10:37, 40).
28: Textual note: Verse 28 is absent from the earliest and most important manuscripts — Sinaiticus (01), Vaticanus (03), Alexandrinus (02), and other significant witnesses. It appears in later manuscripts and reads: kai eplērōthē hē graphē hē legousa, kai meta anomōn elogisthē (and the scripture was fulfilled which says: he was numbered with the transgressors — Isaiah 53:12). Most modern critical editions (NA28, UBS5) place it in a footnote or omit it. It is a correct theological observation about what is happening, but as a verse it is most likely a scribal addition that made explicit what the narrative implies through v.27.
29: blasphēmountes / kinountes tas kephalas autōn / ouah — blaspheming / shaking their heads / Aha! — blasphēmountes (speaking injuriously against, blaspheming) — the same verb used for the charge against Jesus (14:64) is now used by the crowd against Jesus. Kinountes tas kephalas autōn (shaking their heads) echoes Psalm 22:7 LXX: pantes hoi theōrountes me exemykterisan me, elalēsan en cheilesin, ekinēsan kephalēn (all who see me mock me; they shoot out the lip, they shake the head). The Passion narrative is saturated with Psalm 22 echoes. Ouah (Aha! — an exclamation of contemptuous triumph).
30: sōson seauton / katabasas apo tou staurou — save yourself / coming down from the cross — the taunt inverts the Gospel's central paradox: the one who saves others is unable to save himself precisely because he is saving others. Sōson seauton (save yourself) — the imperative uses the same verb (sōzō) that describes Jesus's healing ministry throughout Mark. The crowds unknowingly express the theological truth: self-saving would have meant non-salvation for others.
31: allous esōsen / heauton ou dynatai sōsai — he saved others / he cannot save himself — the chief priests' mockery contains the Gospel in two clauses. Allous esōsen (he saved others — aorist, completed past acts of salvation) — the healings, exorcisms, raising of the dead. Heauton ou dynatai sōsai (he cannot save himself) — the ou dynatai (cannot) uses the same verb-root as dynamos (power) and echoes 10:27 (para anthrōpois adynaton, all' ou para theō, impossible with humans, but not with God). The salvation of others requires the non-salvation of himself.
32: ho Christos ho basileus Israēl / katabasatō nyn / idōmen kai pisteusōmen — the Christ, the King of Israel / come down now / that we may see and believe — ho Christos ho basileus Israēl — the scribes combine the Messianic title with the royal designation, formulating the very truth they deny. Idōmen kai pisteusōmen (that we may see and believe) — the conditional structure inverts the Gospel's logic: faith opens to sight, not sight producing faith. Jesus's refusal to perform a sign-miracle here (cf. 8:11–12) is consistent with his entire ministry's logic. Synestaurōmenoi (those crucified with him — even the two rebels join the mockery in Mark).
33: skotos egeneto eph' holēn tēn gēn / heōs hōras enatēs — darkness came over the whole land / until the ninth hour — skotos (darkness) from noon (hōras hektēs, sixth hour) until 3 PM (hōras enatēs, ninth hour) — three hours. The darkness echoes Amos 8:9: kai estai en tē hēmera ekeinē, legei kyrios, dysetai ho hēlios mesēmbrias (on that day, says the Lord, the sun will go down at noon). Also Exodus 10:21–23 (the darkness plague over Egypt). The darkness is cosmic participation in the death of the Messiah — or in Mark's register, a sign of divine judgment and mourning.
35: Ēlian phōnei — he is calling Elijah — the confusion between Eloi (Aramaic: my God, from El) and Ēlias (Elijah in Greek) is phonetically plausible: both begin with the el- sound. Whether the bystanders genuinely misheard or were mockingly misrepresenting his cry is debated. Elijah was expected to come as a precursor to the end (Mal 4:5; 3:23 LXX); anticipating that Jesus might summon Elijah adds an eschatological layer to the crowd's response.
36: oxous / spongon / perititheis kalamo / aphete idōmen — sour wine / sponge / placing on a reed / let us see — oxos (sour wine, vinegar — cheap wine for soldiers and laborers; different from the myrrh-wine offered in v.23). Psalm 69:21 LXX: edōkan eis to brōma mou cholēn kai eis tēn dipsan mou epotisan me oxos (they gave me gall for my food and vinegar for my thirst). The narrator's comment aphete idōmen ei erchetai Ēlias (let us see if Elijah comes) — another instance of the crowds expecting a miraculous rescue that does not come.
37: apheis phōnēn megalēn exepneusen — letting out a loud voice he breathed his last — exepneusen (he breathed out, expired — from ekpneō: ek- = out + pneō = to breathe; related to pneuma, spirit/breath). The last breath is ordinarily labored and quiet in crucifixion (respiratory failure from hanging). A phōnē megalē (loud cry/voice) at the moment of death is physiologically striking and theologically purposive — it is not a death of exhaustion but a deliberate release. John 19:30 makes the deliberateness explicit: paradōken to pneuma (he handed over the spirit).
38: to katapetasma tou naou eschisthē eis dyo ap' anōthen heōs katō — the veil of the temple was torn into two from top to bottom — katapetasma (veil, curtain) — either the outer curtain of the sanctuary (visible from the temple court) or the inner veil separating the Holy of Holies (accessible only to the High Priest on Yom Kippur, Lev 16). Eschisthē (was torn — aorist passive of schizō) — the same verb used for the heavens being torn at the baptism (1:10: schizomenous tous ouranous). The tearing of the heavens opened at Jesus's identification as Son; the tearing of the veil accompanies his death. Ap' anōthen heōs katō (from top to bottom — the direction of divine rather than human action).
40: apo makrothen / Maria hē Magdalēnē — from far off / Mary the Magdalene — apo makrothen (from a distance) — the women maintain the distance the disciples have also kept (Peter followed apo makrothen, 14:54). Three women are named: Maria hē Magdalēnē (Mary the Magdalene — from Magdala on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee; the Magdalēnē title distinguishes her from the other Marys); Maria hē Iakōbou tou mikrou kai Iōsētos mētēr (Mary the mother of James the small/lesser and of Joseph); Salōmē (named only in Mark among the Synoptics).
41: ēkolouthoun autō kai diēkonoun / gynai pollai — were following him and serving him / many women — ēkolouthoun (were following — imperfect, continuous; the discipleship word) and diēkonoun (were serving/ministering — imperfect; the word for table-service and the word Jesus used for his own mission: diakonēsai, 10:45). Mark retroactively identifies these women as disciples from Galilee using the precise vocabulary of discipleship. Gynai pollai (many women) — their number underscores that the male disciples' flight (14:50) was not the whole story of the community's response to the cross.
42: paraskeuē / prosabbaton — Preparation Day / pre-Sabbath day — paraskeuē (Preparation — Friday, the day of preparation for the Sabbath) is a technical term found in all four Gospels. Prosabbaton (pre-Sabbath day) is a hapax legomenon in the NT (only here), reinforcing the temporal urgency: burial must occur before sunset when the Sabbath begins (Deut 21:23 requires burial of an executed criminal on the day of death).
43: euschēmōn bouleutēs / prosedechomenos tēn basileian tou theou / tolmēsas — a respected council member / awaiting the kingdom of God / daring — euschēmōn (respected, prominent, of good standing — the word for social honor). Bouleutēs (council member — a member of the Sanhedrin, which had just condemned Jesus). Prosdechomenos tēn basileian tou theou (waiting/expecting the kingdom of God) — the phrase identifies Joseph as sympathetic to Jesus's message. Tolmēsas eisēlthen pros ton Pilaton (daring, he went in to Pilate) — tolmaō (to dare, be bold) acknowledges that requesting the body of a crucified rebel was an act of political courage.
44: ethaumasen ei ēdē tethnēken / proskaleis ton kentyriōna — was surprised that he was already dead / summoning the centurion — ethaumasen (was surprised, marveled) — crucifixion typically took 24–72 hours; death in approximately 6 hours was fast (Jesus was crucified at ~9 AM and died at ~3 PM). Pilate's inquiry of the centurion constitutes a de facto death certification — a legal verification of death before releasing the body. The centurion's testimony (v.45) provides the official confirmation.
45: gnous apo tou kentyriōnos / ptōma / edōrēsato tō Iōsēph — having learned from the centurion / the corpse / he gave it as a gift to Joseph — ptōma (corpse, body of a fallen person — a specific word for a dead body, from piptō, to fall). Edōrēsato (gave as a gift — from dōreomai, to grant, bestow; carries the sense of a favor or gift given from a position of authority). The verb dignifies Joseph's receipt: he is not scavenging but receiving an official grant.
46: sindona / katheilon / enetylixen tē sindoni / mnēmeion / lithos — linen cloth / took down / wrapped in the linen cloth / tomb / stone — sindōn (fine linen cloth) — the same word for the young man's garment in 14:51–52; a connection many readers have found significant. Katheilen (took down — technical term for taking down a body from a cross). Enetylixen tē sindoni (wrapped in the linen). The tomb is described as lelatomēmenon ek petras (cut out of rock — a rock-cut tomb); lithos (stone) rolled to the entrance — the stone that will concern the women in 16:3.
47: ethēanto / etethē — they observed / was placed — ethēanto (they beheld, observed — from theaomai, to gaze carefully upon; the root of the English theatre). The two Marys (Maria hē Magdalēnē kai Maria hē Iōsētos) are explicitly noted as witnesses of the burial location. Their observation of pou tethetai (where he was placed) is the narrative link to the resurrection discovery: they know where to find the tomb (16:1–2). The women are the continuity witnesses across burial and resurrection.
